# c-moiwnmoc-bb598749904c [live]
> Design Reckoner dependency-aware consideration (Option A) — Reckoner reads outbound depends-on links during consideration; if any direct target is non-terminal, defer the petition with defer_reason='dependency' instead of accepting. Resolves concern 3 of c-moivk7pd (WIP-cap deadlock): without this, the Reckoner can accept a batch of dependent writs whose dependencies are still pending petitions, fill the WIP cap with gated writs sitting in 'open', and starve the dependencies of acceptance capacity. With this, dependents stay in 'new' (don't count against WIP) until their dependencies clear, naturally preserving topological accept order. Open questions: (1) wake-up semantics — re-evaluate every tick (cheap, polling matches scheduling-trigger conclusion c-mod9a54n) or fire on writ-completion CDC events (more responsive, requires defer_signal infrastructure from c-modaqnpt)? (2) which target phases count as 'cleared' — Spider's existing logic treats {completed, cancelled} as released and {failed} as cascade-to-stuck; should the Reckoner mirror the same set, or use writ-type-config classification ('terminal' + 'success' attr)? (3) dangling references — Spider treats missing targets as still-gated; Reckoner should match. (4) cycle handling — Spider already detects cycles in the depends-on graph; Reckoner consideration should also surface cycles before accepting, or rely on Spider to fail-loud post-accept? Coordinates with the depends-on rename (sibling click) for the link-kind reading; short-term Reckoner can read 'spider.follows' until the rename lands.
Status: live
Created by: ae7f4ae1-3b00-4a84-a8d5-35d1be7f1aba
Created: 2026-04-28T17:33:47.532Z

content.content:
# c-moivk7pd-7bedba64b71a [concluded]
> Lifecycle interactions between Reckoner gating, arbitrary writ types' custom lifecycles, and inter-writ relationships. Reckoner uses new→open as the accept transition — this works cleanly for mandates but assumes the writ type's active phase is named 'open'. How does this generalize to writ types with custom lifecycles (e.g. cartograph charges/pieces have draft|active|... stages and may have non-'open' first active phases)? Second concern: there is currently no way to DRAFT a writ before sending it to the petitioner other than omitting ext.reckoner — once ext is set, the writ is live for Reckoner consideration. This means parent/child or 'follows' dependency relationships cannot be set up before dispatch without risking the target writ being accepted and dispatched before its dependents are wired. Third concern: deadlock potential — if the Reckoner has a WIP limit and accepts a batch of writs that all 'follows'-depend on unaccepted petitions still queued, the accepted writs sit blocked waiting on petitions that may never get capacity to be considered. Need to think through whether a draft-state-before-petition mechanism is needed, whether dependencies should be Reckoner-aware (defer accept until dependencies are clear), and how active-phase naming generalizes per writ-type.
Status: concluded
Conclusion: Three lifecycle-interaction concerns resolved with three follow-up children opened.

**Concern 1 (active-phase naming) — already solved at the implementation level.** The Reckoner's resolveActiveTargetPhase walks the writ-type's registered state machine, finds states reachable from the current phase whose classification is 'active', picks the unique candidate, and fail-louds on 0 or 2+. It does NOT hardcode 'open'. For mandate/vision/charge/piece (all six-state new→open→...) it picks 'open' automatically; for a future writ type with new→active it would pick 'active'. The unstated invariant — 'a Reckoner-gated writ type must have exactly one initial→active transition' — is enforced at runtime via fail-loud; no need to lift to writ-type-registration validation in v0. Revisit if/when a second non-mandate writ type opts into Reckoner gating.

**Concern 2 (drafting before petitioning) — workable today, ergonomics gap to close.** The Reckoner gates on ext.reckoner being present (not phase alone), and re-fires on ext.reckoner change (D14). So the draft idiom is: create writ in 'new' WITHOUT ext.reckoner (invisible to Reckoner), set up parent/child + depends-on links, then patch ext.reckoner to publish. Atomic multi-writ setup composes with Stacks transactions. Cross-apparatus authoring (apparatus X creates A, apparatus Y wants B-follows-A) remains an exotic case parked for now. Ergonomics: the publish step needs a first-class API. Follow-up child c-moiwnb9i opens reckoner.petition(writId, ext) helper.

**Concern 3 (WIP-cap deadlock) — Option A: Reckoner-aware dependencies.** Without intervention, the Reckoner can accept a batch of dependent writs whose dependencies are still pending petitions, fill the WIP cap with gated writs in 'open', and starve the dependencies. Resolution: Reckoner reads outbound depends-on links during consideration; if any direct target is non-terminal, defer the petition with defer_reason='dependency' (slots into c-modaqnpt's existing defer machinery). Deferred writs stay in 'new', don't count against WIP, capacity is preserved for the dependencies. Rejected Option B (topological priority bonus — only shifts probability, doesn't eliminate the deadlock), Option C (Spider flags gated writs back to Reckoner — violates c-mof657j4's no-cross-layer-coordination principle), Option D (decline-and-re-emit — pushes bookkeeping onto petitioners, hostile to recursive vision-keeper cascades). Follow-up child c-moiwnmoc opens the design detail (wake-up semantics, target-phase semantics, cycle handling).

**Coupled decision: rename spider.follows → depends-on, Clerk-contributed (unprefixed).** Once the Reckoner reads the dependency link, it's no longer Spider-private. Renaming captures the framework-neutral intent ('B blocked until A reaches terminal-success'). Establishes a broader naming-primacy principle: foundational plugins that own a substrate (Clerk owns the writ graph) get the unique privilege of contributing unprefixed link kinds; everything else is plugin-namespaced (spider.*, astrolabe.*, cartograph.*). Follow-up child c-moiwnzw6 owns the rename + the primacy-principle codification.

Children opened: c-moiwnb9i (reckoner.petition helper), c-moiwnmoc (Reckoner dependency-aware consideration), c-moiwnzw6 (depends-on rename + Clerk naming primacy).
Resolved by: ae7f4ae1-3b00-4a84-a8d5-35d1be7f1aba
Created: 2026-04-28T17:03:08.545Z
Resolved: 2026-04-28T17:34:33.989Z

## c-moiwnb9i-391a66734c2b [live]
> Add reckoner.petition(writId, ext) helper that publishes a draft writ by patching ext.reckoner onto an already-created writ. Resolves concern 2 of c-moivk7pd (drafting before petitioning): the existing draft idiom — create writ in 'new' phase WITHOUT ext.reckoner, set up parent/child links and depends-on relationships, then patch ext.reckoner to publish — works mechanically but is undiscoverable. The helper makes the publish step a first-class Reckoner API: takes a writ id and the ext payload (source, priority, complexity), patches the writ via Clerk, fires the existing CDC re-evaluation gate (D14, ext.reckoner change). Atomic multi-writ setup remains: callers wrap create + link + petition() in a Stacks transaction. Worked example: vision-keeper creates a charge writ in 'new' (no ext), creates depends-on link to its review-vision parent, calls reckoner.petition(chargeId, {...}). Open: shape of the ext argument (typed ReckonerExt vs raw object), idempotency on double-publish (no-op if ext already set vs error), patch-vs-replace semantics if ext is partially populated.
Status: live
Created by: ae7f4ae1-3b00-4a84-a8d5-35d1be7f1aba
Created: 2026-04-28T17:33:32.742Z

## c-moiwnzw6-4a2801d08a34 [live]
> Rename spider.follows → depends-on as a Clerk-contributed (unprefixed) link kind, and establish the principle that the Clerk plugin holds primacy of naming for framework-level link kinds. Two coupled decisions: (1) The semantic relationship 'B is blocked until A reaches a terminal-success phase' is fundamental to writ-graph structure, not Spider-internal — Reckoner needs to read it for dependency-aware consideration (sibling click), Spider continues to gate dispatch on it, and any future apparatus reasoning about writ ordering reads the same edge. Renaming to 'depends-on' captures the intent in framework-neutral terms. (2) The Clerk plugin is the framework writ-graph owner and gets the unique privilege of contributing link kinds without a plugin prefix. Plugin-namespaced link kinds (spider.*, astrolabe.*, cartograph.*) remain the default — those plugins own private edges. Unprefixed kinds (depends-on, possibly others later) are Clerk-contributed and represent universal writ-graph semantics every writ-aware operator can rely on. This establishes a 'primacy of naming' convention: foundational plugins that own a substrate get an unprefixed namespace within that substrate. Migration shape: introduce 'depends-on' as a Clerk-registered link kind, add the new kind to Spider's gate-evaluation reader (accept either kind during transition), backfill data via a one-shot CDC migration, retire the spider.follows reads. Open: exact namespace mechanism (does the Clerk register link kinds with a registry the way it registers writ types? or are link kinds free-form strings with the prefix convention purely a soft rule?), audit/enforce no-collision rule between Clerk's namespace and plugin-prefixed kinds, doc placement for the primacy principle (likely guild-vocabulary tome plus framework architecture doc).
Status: live
Created by: ae7f4ae1-3b00-4a84-a8d5-35d1be7f1aba
Created: 2026-04-28T17:34:04.663Z

## c-moix6ovo-404015d0e1bb [live]
> Manually migrate existing WritLinkDoc rows from kind='spider.follows' to kind='depends-on' in the live guild's link store, after the rename commission (w-moix2b56-19767872adb9) ships. Out-of-band operational work — the dispatch pipeline has no guild-database access, so the migration is performed directly by Coco/Sean against the live stacks DB rather than included in the artificer's commission. Trigger: as soon as possible after the rename writ lands, to minimize the window where existing 'spider.follows' edges are orphaned (Spider's gate evaluation will be filtering on 'depends-on' and find no edges; Astrolabe's observation-lift will be writing 'depends-on' going forward; legacy rows sit unreferenced until migrated). Open: exact mechanism (direct stacks DB patch via Book.patch on the writ-links book? a small script? CLI invocation?), whether to pre-create 'depends-on' rows alongside the existing 'spider.follows' rows before the rename ships (closes the orphan window entirely, but adds a coordination step), and validation step after migration (count of 'spider.follows' rows should be zero, count of 'depends-on' rows should match the prior 'spider.follows' count). Note that link doc ids are computed from (sourceId, targetId, label) — the kind is a mutable field on the existing row, so migration is an in-place patch, not a delete-and-recreate.
Status: live
Created by: ae7f4ae1-3b00-4a84-a8d5-35d1be7f1aba
Created: 2026-04-28T17:48:36.852Z

content.text:
Got the click loaded plus the surrounding context (parent c-moivk7pd, scheduling-trigger conclusion c-mod9a54n, defer-metadata conclusion c-modaqnpt, sibling tick design c-moiw5wkv). Here's where I land on the four open questions, with reasoning — then we can decide whether to draft this as a design doc in `.scratch/`.

## The mechanism, in one paragraph

On each tick, for every pending petition, the Reckoner walks the writ's outbound `depends-on` links (reading `spider.follows` until the rename lands). If any direct target's current phase is non-terminal-success, the petition is deferred with `defer_reason='dependency'`. Deferred petitions stay in the writ's `new` phase, don't count against WIP. Next tick re-evaluates from scratch (stateless). Dependents naturally release in topological order as their dependencies reach terminal-success.

## Q1 — Wake-up semantics: **polling, no CDC**

This was settled in **c-mod9a54n** ("v0 Reckoner runs on a fixed-interval polling tick — no event-driven triggers... Rejected event-driven triggers as premature mechanism for MVP"). Dependency wake-up is just another defer-reason that gets re-evaluated each tick — it doesn't deserve a special path.

c-modaqnpt's mention of "triggered by writ-completion events" is the v0-reserved `defer_signal` field, not active wiring. Worst-case latency is one tick (60s default) between a dependency completing and a dependent being accepted. For the dependency-resolution use case, that's fine — we're not blocking patron interaction, we're unblocking the next layer of an autonomous dependency graph.

If polling-induced latency ever becomes a real problem, c-modaqnpt left `defer_signal` ready to wire up.

## Q2 — Phase classification: **mirror Spider, but use writ-type-config**

The semantic of `depends-on` (per the parent conclusion) is *"B blocked until A reaches a terminal-success phase"*. Three cases for a target:

- **Terminal + success** (e.g. mandate `completed`, vision `approved`): release — petition becomes acceptable.
- **Terminal + non-success** (e.g. `failed`, `cancelled`): the dependency is unsatisfiable. **Auto-decline** the petition with `defer_reason='dependency_failed'` (or a new `decline_reason`). Don't leave it deferred to rot — propagate the failure cascade to the petitioner, who can re-petition with revised dependencies if appropriate. *(This diverges slightly from Spider, which cascades accepted writs to `stuck`. The Reckoner is at the petition layer, so the analogous move is decline-with-reason.)*
- **Non-terminal** (still in `new`/`open`/active): defer.

I recommend reading classification from **writ-type-config phase metadata** rather than hard-coding `{completed, cancelled}` — it's how `resolveActiveTargetPhase` already works (per c-moivk7pd), and it generalizes cleanly when new writ types onboard. Concretely: a phase needs both `terminal: true` and `success: true` to count as "released". One open subquestion: is `cancelled` success or failure? Spider treats it as released (success-shaped). I'd argue the same — cancellation is intentional and the dependent petition has no semantic reason to fail just because someone cancelled the dependency. Worth confirming with Sean.

## Q3 — Dangling references: **mirror Spider, treat as still-gated**

Adopt Spider's behavior verbatim. Missing target = defer (not decline). Reasons:
- A patron may create a dependent before its dependency is created (out-of-order authoring).
- Stacks consistency: a freshly-created target may not yet be visible to a follower.
- Treating dangling as decline pushes failure semantics onto a transient condition.

The staleness diagnostic in Q4 catches *persistently* dangling references.

## Q4 — Cycles: **don't detect explicitly; rely on deferral + staleness diagnostic**

Cycle detection in the Reckoner hot path duplicates Spider's work and isn't needed for safety. If A→B→A, both petitions evaluate as "dependency non-terminal" and stay deferred. The WIP cap is preserved (the whole point of Option A). The Reckoner doesn't deadlock — the *cycle* is stuck, but the system isn't.

Instead, add a **deferred-petition staleness diagnostic**: any petition with `defer_count > N` or `first_deferred_at > T ago` gets surfaced (Lattice pulse, evaluation log, or vision-keeper notification — TBD). This single mechanism catches cycles, dangling-forever references, dead dependencies, and any other "stuck in deferred" pathology — without coupling the Reckoner to Spider's cycle detector.

Spider continues to fail-loud on cycles post-accept as a defense-in-depth check, but it should rarely trigger if the Reckoner is doing its job (since Reckoner-deferred petitions never reach accept).

---

## Recommendation

Draft this as a design doc in `.scratch/reckoner/dependency-aware-consideration.md` covering:

1. The mechanism (the one-paragraph summary above, expanded with pseudocode)
2. The four resolutions above
3. Coordination notes with the depends-on rename (sibling click) and `defer_reason` enum extension (`dependency`, `dependency_failed`)
4. Open items to flag back to you: (a) cancelled-as-success confirmation, (b) auto-decline vs. perpetual-defer for failed deps, (c) staleness diagnostic surface area

Want me to draft it? Or would you rather push on any of these resolutions first — I'm genuinely uncertain on the cancelled-as-success question and the auto-decline-on-failure call, and either of those could shift the doc's shape.

# Reckoner dependency-aware consideration (Option A)

**Click:** c-moiwnmoc
**Parent:** c-moivk7pd (resolved Concern 3 — WIP-cap deadlock)
**Coordinates with:** c-moiwnzw6 (depends-on rename), c-moiw5wkv (tick relay), c-modaqnpt (defer metadata)

## Problem

Without dependency-aware consideration, the Reckoner can accept a batch of dependent writs whose dependencies are still pending petitions, fill the WIP cap with gated writs sitting in `open`, and starve the dependencies of acceptance capacity. The accepted-but-blocked writs hold WIP slots indefinitely while the dependencies they need can't get past the petition gate.

**Resolution shape (Option A from c-moivk7pd):** the Reckoner reads outbound `depends-on` links during consideration. If any direct target is not yet *cleared*, the petition is deferred (writ stays in `new`, doesn't count against WIP). Dependents naturally release in topological order as their dependencies clear.

## Mechanism

On each Reckoner tick, for every pending petition:

1. Load the writ's outbound `depends-on` links via the Clerk's link store.
   - **Short-term:** read `kind = 'spider.follows'`.
   - **Post-rename:** read `kind = 'depends-on'` (per c-moiwnzw6).
   - **Transition window:** read either kind (Spider's gate evaluator already does this).
2. For each target writ:
   - Resolve the target's current phase.
   - Look up the phase's classification in the writ-type config.
   - Classify as `cleared`, `failed`, or `gating` (see Q2 below).
3. Aggregate:
   - All targets `cleared` → petition is eligible (proceed to normal acceptance evaluation).
   - Any target `gating` → defer with `defer_reason = 'dependency_pending'`.
   - At least one target `failed` and no targets `gating` → defer with `defer_reason = 'dependency_failed'`.
   - Mixed `failed` + `gating` → defer with `defer_reason = 'dependency_pending'` (the gating dep might still resolve favorably; failed-dep signal surfaces only once the gate set is fully terminal).
4. Emit a Reckonings row for every consideration outcome (existing infra from c-modc7m16).

The pass is stateless — same inputs always produce same outputs. Re-evaluation each tick reuses this logic with no carry-over.

## Resolutions to open questions

### Q1 — Wake-up semantics: **polling, no CDC**

**Decision:** dependency-resolved petitions wake up on the next Reckoner tick. No event-driven triggers.

**Rationale:** c-mod9a54n already concluded that the v0 Reckoner runs on a fixed-interval polling tick — no event-driven triggers — for substrate uniformity with Spider. Dependency wake-up is just another defer reason that gets re-evaluated each tick; it doesn't deserve a special path.

c-modaqnpt's `defer_signal` field is reserved for a future event-based wake-up but is not wired in v0. Worst-case latency is one tick (60s default) between a dependency clearing and a dependent being accepted. For autonomous dependency-graph progression, that's acceptable — we're not blocking patron interaction.

If polling-induced latency becomes a real problem, `defer_signal` is ready to wire up later.

### Q2 — Phase classification: **mirror Spider, but read from writ-type config**

**Terminology:** a target phase is *cleared* if it's classified as terminal-success — i.e. terminal **and** considered a successful conclusion. Plainly, "the dependency completed successfully (or was cancelled cleanly — see below)." Anything else is either *failed* (terminal-non-success) or *gating* (non-terminal).

**Decision:** read phase classification from writ-type config rather than hard-coding `{completed, cancelled}`. A phase counts as cleared if it has both `terminal: true` and `success: true` in its writ-type-config classification. This generalizes cleanly when new writ types onboard, and is consistent with how `resolveActiveTargetPhase` already works (per c-moivk7pd's resolution of Concern 1).

**Cancellation as success:** for v0, treat `cancelled` as terminal-success (mirroring Spider's existing released set). Cancelling a dependency is intentional, and there's no semantic reason for a dependent petition to be poisoned just because someone cancelled the dependency. Revisit if a real workflow demands distinguishing cancelled-as-success from cancelled-as-abandonment.

**No auto-decline on failed dependencies (recoverability over termination).** Even when a target is terminally failed, the Reckoner does **not** decline the dependent petition. Instead, it defers with `defer_reason = 'dependency_failed'`. The dependent writ stays in `new` (recoverable), the petitioner gets a clear signal via the Reckonings log and the staleness diagnostic, and recovery options remain open:

- The petitioner re-petitions after fixing dependencies (e.g. swapping the failed dep for a substitute, retrying the dep, or removing the link).
- The petitioner withdraws the petition (terminal, petitioner-initiated — already in c-modaqnpt's lifecycle).
- An operator inspects and resolves out-of-band.

Auto-declining was rejected because it terminates the petition (per c-modaqnpt: declined is terminal) and forces re-creation rather than re-petition. The dependent's intent is preserved; only its scheduling is paused.

**`defer_reason` enum extension** (additions to c-modaqnpt's set):

- `dependency_pending` — at least one target is still gating; check again next tick.
- `dependency_failed` — all targets terminal, but at least one failed; staleness diagnostic should escalate fast.

### Q3 — Dangling references: **mirror Spider, treat as still-gated**

**Decision:** if a `depends-on` target writ doesn't resolve, defer with `defer_reason = 'dependency_pending'`. Adopt Spider's existing behavior verbatim.

**Rationale:**
- A patron may create a dependent before its dependency exists (out-of-order authoring, especially with multi-writ Stacks transactions).
- A freshly-created target may not yet be visible to a follower due to consistency lag.
- Treating dangling as decline (or even as `dependency_failed`) bakes failure semantics into a transient condition.

The staleness diagnostic in Q4 catches *persistently* dangling references.

### Q4 — Cycles: **no explicit detection; rely on deferral + staleness diagnostic**

**Decision:** the Reckoner does not detect cycles in its consideration pass. Cycles are handled implicitly by the deferral mechanism (every member of a cycle has a non-terminal dep → every member stays deferred → WIP cap is preserved).

Spider continues to fail-loud on cycles post-accept as defense-in-depth, but it should rarely trigger if the Reckoner is doing its job (deferred petitions never reach accept).

**Safety net:** a separate **deferred-petition staleness diagnostic** surfaces any petition with `defer_count > N` or `first_deferred_at > T ago`. This single diagnostic catches:

- Cycles (members defer forever).
- Persistently dangling references (target never appears).
- `dependency_failed` petitions awaiting petitioner action.
- Any other "stuck in deferred" pathology.

**Status:** this mechanism does not yet exist. New click to be opened (see Coordination below).

## Coordination

- **c-moiwnzw6 (depends-on rename):** the Reckoner can land first reading `spider.follows`. Switch to `depends-on` reads after the rename; during the migration window, read either kind (Spider already does this — share the helper).
- **c-moiw5wkv (tick relay):** the dependency check is part of the per-petition evaluation invoked by the tick relay. No new infrastructure needed beyond extending the evaluator.
- **c-modaqnpt (defer metadata):** add `dependency_pending` and `dependency_failed` to the `defer_reason` enum. No other shape changes.
- **New click — staleness diagnostic:** open a sibling click to design the deferred-petition staleness surface (Lattice pulse? Reckonings query? vision-keeper notification? threshold config?). Q4's correctness depends on this existing eventually; without it, `dependency_failed` and cycle pathologies have no escalation path.

## Open items flagged back to Sean

1. **Cancelled-as-success** — confirmed for v0 (matches Spider). Doc reflects this.
2. **No auto-decline on failed deps** — confirmed; recovery preference drove this. Doc reflects this.
3. **Staleness diagnostic** — new click to be opened (Sean's call).

## Sketch of the consideration loop

```
for petition in pending_petitions:
  links = clerk.linksFrom(petition.writId, kind in ['spider.follows', 'depends-on'])
  if links.isEmpty:
    proceedToCapacityCheck(petition)
    continue

# Reckoner dependency-aware consideration (Option A)

**Click:** c-moiwnmoc
**Parent:** c-moivk7pd (resolved Concern 3 — WIP-cap deadlock)
**Coordinates with:** c-moiwnzw6 (depends-on rename), c-moiw5wkv (tick relay), c-modaqnpt (defer metadata)

## Problem

Without dependency-aware consideration, the Reckoner can accept a batch of dependent writs whose dependencies are still pending petitions, fill the WIP cap with gated writs sitting in `open`, and starve the dependencies of acceptance capacity. The accepted-but-blocked writs hold WIP slots indefinitely while the dependencies they need can't get past the petition gate.

**Resolution shape (Option A from c-moivk7pd):** the Reckoner reads outbound `depends-on` links during consideration. If any direct target is not yet *cleared*, the petition is deferred (writ stays in `new`, doesn't count against WIP). Dependents naturally release in topological order as their dependencies clear.

## Mechanism

On each Reckoner tick, for every pending petition:

1. Load the writ's outbound `depends-on` links via the Clerk's link store.
   - **Short-term:** read `kind = 'spider.follows'`.
   - **Post-rename:** read `kind = 'depends-on'` (per c-moiwnzw6).
   - **Transition window:** read either kind (Spider's gate evaluator already does this).
2. For each target writ:
   - Resolve the target's current phase.
   - Look up the phase's classification in the writ-type config.
   - Classify as `cleared`, `failed`, or `gating` (see Q2 below).
3. Aggregate:
   - All targets `cleared` → petition is eligible (proceed to normal acceptance evaluation).
   - Any target `gating` → defer with `defer_reason = 'dependency_pending'`.
   - At least one target `failed` and no targets `gating` → defer with `defer_reason = 'dependency_failed'`.
   - Mixed `failed` + `gating` → defer with `defer_reason = 'dependency_pending'` (the gating dep might still resolve favorably; failed-dep signal surfaces only once the gate set is fully terminal).
4. Emit a Reckonings row for every consideration outcome (existing infra from c-modc7m16).

The pass is stateless — same inputs always produce same outputs. Re-evaluation each tick reuses this logic with no carry-over.

## Resolutions to open questions

### Q1 — Wake-up semantics: **polling, no CDC**

**Decision:** dependency-resolved petitions wake up on the next Reckoner tick. No event-driven triggers.

**Rationale:** c-mod9a54n already concluded that the v0 Reckoner runs on a fixed-interval polling tick — no event-driven triggers — for substrate uniformity with Spider. Dependency wake-up is just another defer reason that gets re-evaluated each tick; it doesn't deserve a special path.

c-modaqnpt's `defer_signal` field is reserved for a future event-based wake-up but is not wired in v0. Worst-case latency is one tick (60s default) between a dependency clearing and a dependent being accepted. For autonomous dependency-graph progression, that's acceptable — we're not blocking patron interaction.

If polling-induced latency becomes a real problem, `defer_signal` is ready to wire up later.

### Q2 — Phase classification: **mirror Spider, but read from writ-type config**

**Terminology:** a target phase is *cleared* if it's classified as terminal-success — i.e. terminal **and** considered a successful conclusion. Plainly, "the dependency completed successfully (or was cancelled cleanly — see below)." Anything else is either *failed* (terminal-non-success) or *gating* (non-terminal).

**Decision:** read phase classification from writ-type config rather than hard-coding `{completed, cancelled}`. A phase counts as cleared if it has both `terminal: true` and `success: true` in its writ-type-config classification. This generalizes cleanly when new writ types onboard, and is consistent with how `resolveActiveTargetPhase` already works (per c-moivk7pd's resolution of Concern 1).

**Cancellation as success:** for v0, treat `cancelled` as terminal-success (mirroring Spider's existing released set). Cancelling a dependency is intentional, and there's no semantic reason for a dependent petition to be poisoned just because someone cancelled the dependency. Revisit if a real workflow demands distinguishing cancelled-as-success from cancelled-as-abandonment.

**No auto-decline on failed dependencies (recoverability over termination).** Even when a target is terminally failed, the Reckoner does **not** decline the dependent petition. Instead, it defers with `defer_reason = 'dependency_failed'`. The dependent writ stays in `new` (recoverable), the petitioner gets a clear signal via the Reckonings log and the staleness diagnostic, and recovery options remain open:

- The petitioner re-petitions after fixing dependencies (e.g. swapping the failed dep for a substitute, retrying the dep, or removing the link).
- The petitioner withdraws the petition (terminal, petitioner-initiated — already in c-modaqnpt's lifecycle).
- An operator inspects and resolves out-of-band.

Auto-declining was rejected because it terminates the petition (per c-modaqnpt: declined is terminal) and forces re-creation rather than re-petition. The dependent's intent is preserved; only its scheduling is paused.

**`defer_reason` enum extension** (additions to c-modaqnpt's set):

- `dependency_pending` — at least one target is still gating; check again next tick.
- `dependency_failed` — all targets terminal, but at least one failed; staleness diagnostic should escalate fast.

### Q3 — Dangling references: **mirror Spider, treat as still-gated**

**Decision:** if a `depends-on` target writ doesn't resolve, defer with `defer_reason = 'dependency_pending'`. Adopt Spider's existing behavior verbatim.

**Rationale:**
- A patron may create a dependent before its dependency exists (out-of-order authoring, especially with multi-writ Stacks transactions).
- A freshly-created target may not yet be visible to a follower due to consistency lag.
- Treating dangling as decline (or even as `dependency_failed`) bakes failure semantics into a transient condition.

The staleness diagnostic in Q4 catches *persistently* dangling references.

**Decision:** the Reckoner does not detect cycles in its consideration pass. Cycles are handled implicitly by the deferral mechanism (every member of a cycle has a non-terminal dep → every member stays deferred → WIP cap is preserved).

Spider continues to fail-loud on cycles post-accept as defense-in-depth, but it should rarely trigger if the Reckoner is doing its job (deferred petitions never reach accept).

**Safety net:** a separate **deferred-petition staleness diagnostic** surfaces any petition with `defer_count > N` or `first_deferred_at > T ago`. This single diagnostic catches:

- Cycles (members defer forever).
- Persistently dangling references (target never appears).
- `dependency_failed` petitions awaiting petitioner action.
- Any other "stuck in deferred" pathology.

**Status:** this mechanism does not yet exist. New click to be opened (see Coordination below).

## Coordination

- **c-moiwnzw6 (depends-on rename):** the Reckoner can land first reading `spider.follows`. Switch to `depends-on` reads after the rename; during the migration window, read either kind (Spider already does this — share the helper).

- **c-modaqnpt (defer metadata):** add `dependency_pending` and `dependency_failed` to the `defer_reason` enum. No other shape changes.
- **New click — staleness diagnostic:** open a sibling click to design the deferred-petition staleness surface (Lattice pulse? Reckonings query? vision-keeper notification? threshold config?). 1. **Cancelled-as-success** — confirmed for v0 (matches Spider). Doc reflects this.

  else:

# Reckoner dependency-aware consideration (Option A)

**Click:** c-moiwnmoc
**Parent:** c-moivk7pd (resolved Concern 3 — WIP-cap deadlock)
**Coordinates with:** c-moiw5wkv (tick relay), c-modaqnpt (defer metadata)
**Sequencing assumption:** the depends-on rename (c-moiwnzw6) lands before this work begins implementation, so the Reckoner reads `kind = 'depends-on'` from the start.

## Problem

Without dependency-aware consideration, the Reckoner can accept a batch of dependent writs whose dependencies are still pending petitions, fill the WIP cap with gated writs sitting in `open`, and starve the dependencies of acceptance capacity. The accepted-but-blocked writs hold WIP slots indefinitely while the dependencies they need can't get past the petition gate.

**Resolution shape (Option A from c-moivk7pd):** the Reckoner reads outbound `depends-on` links during consideration. If any direct target is not yet *cleared*, the petition is deferred (writ stays in `new`, doesn't count against WIP). Dependents naturally release in topological order as their dependencies clear.

## Mechanism

On each Reckoner tick, for every pending petition:

1. Load the writ's outbound `depends-on` links via the Clerk's link store.
   - **Short-term:** read `kind = 'spider.follows'`.
   - **Post-rename:** read `kind = 'depends-on'` (per c-moiwnzw6).
   - **Transition window:** read either kind (Spider's gate evaluator already does this).
2. For each target writ:
   - Resolve the target's current phase.
   - Look up the phase's classification in the writ-type config.
   - Classify as `cleared`, `failed`, or `gating` (see Q2 below).
3. Aggregate:
   - All targets `cleared` → petition is eligible (proceed to normal acceptance evaluation).
   - Any target `gating` → defer with `defer_reason = 'dependency_pending'`.
   - At least one target `failed` and no targets `gating` → defer with `defer_reason = 'dependency_failed'`.
   - Mixed `failed` + `gating` → defer with `defer_reason = 'dependency_pending'` (the gating dep might still resolve favorably; failed-dep signal surfaces only once the gate set is fully terminal).
4. Emit a Reckonings row for every consideration outcome (existing infra from c-modc7m16).

The pass is stateless — same inputs always produce same outputs. Re-evaluation each tick reuses this logic with no carry-over.

## Resolutions to open questions

### Q1 — Wake-up semantics: **polling, no CDC**

**Decision:** dependency-resolved petitions wake up on the next Reckoner tick. No event-driven triggers.

**Rationale:** c-mod9a54n already concluded that the v0 Reckoner runs on a fixed-interval polling tick — no event-driven triggers — for substrate uniformity with Spider. Dependency wake-up is just another defer reason that gets re-evaluated each tick; it doesn't deserve a special path.

c-modaqnpt's `defer_signal` field is reserved for a future event-based wake-up but is not wired in v0. Worst-case latency is one tick (60s default) between a dependency clearing and a dependent being accepted. For autonomous dependency-graph progression, that's acceptable — we're not blocking patron interaction.

If polling-induced latency becomes a real problem, `defer_signal` is ready to wire up later.

### Q2 — Phase classification: **mirror Spider, but read from writ-type config**

**Terminology:** a target phase is *cleared* if it's classified as terminal-success — i.e. terminal **and** considered a successful conclusion. Plainly, "the dependency completed successfully (or was cancelled cleanly — see below)." Anything else is either *failed* (terminal-non-success) or *gating* (non-terminal).

**Decision:** read phase classification from writ-type config rather than hard-coding `{completed, cancelled}`. A phase counts as cleared if it has both `terminal: true` and `success: true` in its writ-type-config classification. This generalizes cleanly when new writ types onboard, and is consistent with how `resolveActiveTargetPhase` already works (per c-moivk7pd's resolution of Concern 1).

**Cancellation as success:** for v0, treat `cancelled` as terminal-success (mirroring Spider's existing released set). Cancelling a dependency is intentional, and there's no semantic reason for a dependent petition to be poisoned just because someone cancelled the dependency. Revisit if a real workflow demands distinguishing cancelled-as-success from cancelled-as-abandonment.

**No auto-decline on failed dependencies (recoverability over termination).** Even when a target is terminally failed, the Reckoner does **not** decline the dependent petition. Instead, it defers with `defer_reason = 'dependency_failed'`. The dependent writ stays in `new` (recoverable), the petitioner gets a clear signal via the Reckonings log and the staleness diagnostic, and recovery options remain open:

- The petitioner re-petitions after fixing dependencies (e.g. swapping the failed dep for a substitute, retrying the dep, or removing the link).
- The petitioner withdraws the petition (terminal, petitioner-initiated — already in c-modaqnpt's lifecycle).
- An operator inspects and resolves out-of-band.

Auto-declining was rejected because it terminates the petition (per c-modaqnpt: declined is terminal) and forces re-creation rather than re-petition. The dependent's intent is preserved; only its scheduling is paused.

**`defer_reason` enum extension** (additions to c-modaqnpt's set):

- `dependency_pending` — at least one target is still gating; check again next tick.
- `dependency_failed` — all targets terminal, but at least one failed; staleness diagnostic should escalate fast.

### Q3 — Dangling references: **mirror Spider, treat as still-gated**

**Decision:** if a `depends-on` target writ doesn't resolve, defer with `defer_reason = 'dependency_pending'`. Adopt Spider's existing behavior verbatim.

**Rationale:**
- A patron may create a dependent before its dependency exists (out-of-order authoring, especially with multi-writ Stacks transactions).
- A freshly-created target may not yet be visible to a follower due to consistency lag.
- Treating dangling as decline (or even as `dependency_failed`) bakes failure semantics into a transient condition.

- Persistently dangling references (target never appears).
- `dependency_failed` petitions awaiting petitioner action.
- Any other "stuck in deferred" pathology.

**Status:** this mechanism does not yet exist. New click to be opened (see Coordination below).

# Reckoner dependency-aware consideration (Option A)

**Click:** c-moiwnmoc
**Parent:** c-moivk7pd (resolved Concern 3 — WIP-cap deadlock)
**Coordinates with:** c-moiw5wkv (tick relay), c-modaqnpt (defer metadata)
**Sequencing assumption:** the depends-on rename (c-moiwnzw6) lands before this work begins implementation, so the Reckoner reads `kind = 'depends-on'` from the start.

## Problem

Without dependency-aware consideration, the Reckoner can accept a batch of dependent writs whose dependencies are still pending petitions, fill the WIP cap with gated writs sitting in `open`, and starve the dependencies of acceptance capacity. The accepted-but-blocked writs hold WIP slots indefinitely while the dependencies they need can't get past the petition gate.

**Resolution shape (Option A from c-moivk7pd):** the Reckoner reads outbound `depends-on` links during consideration. If any direct target is not yet *cleared*, the petition is deferred (writ stays in `new`, doesn't count against WIP). Dependents naturally release in topological order as their dependencies clear.

## Mechanism

On each Reckoner tick, for every pending petition:

1. Load the writ's outbound `depends-on` links via the Clerk's link store (`kind = 'depends-on'`).
2. For each target writ:
   - Resolve the target's current phase.
   - Look up the phase's classification in the writ-type config.
   - Classify as `cleared`, `failed`, or `gating` (see Q2 below).
3. Aggregate (failed-state takes precedence to surface problems faster):
   - All targets `cleared` → petition is eligible (proceed to normal acceptance evaluation).
   - Any target `failed` → defer with `defer_reason = 'dependency_failed'` (regardless of whether other targets are still gating; we want the failed-dep signal to surface immediately for petitioner action).
   - All remaining targets `gating` (none failed) → defer with `defer_reason = 'dependency_pending'`.
4. Emit a Reckonings row for every consideration outcome (existing infra from c-modc7m16).

The pass is stateless — same inputs always produce same outputs. Re-evaluation each tick reuses this logic with no carry-over.

## Resolutions to open questions

### Q1 — Wake-up semantics: **polling, no CDC**

**Decision:** dependency-resolved petitions wake up on the next Reckoner tick. No event-driven triggers.

**Rationale:** c-mod9a54n already concluded that the v0 Reckoner runs on a fixed-interval polling tick — no event-driven triggers — for substrate uniformity with Spider. Dependency wake-up is just another defer reason that gets re-evaluated each tick; it doesn't deserve a special path.

c-modaqnpt's `defer_signal` field is reserved for a future event-based wake-up but is not wired in v0. Worst-case latency is one tick (60s default) between a dependency clearing and a dependent being accepted. For autonomous dependency-graph progression, that's acceptable — we're not blocking patron interaction.

If polling-induced latency becomes a real problem, `defer_signal` is ready to wire up later.

### Q2 — Phase classification: **mirror Spider, but read from writ-type config**

**Terminology:** a target phase is *cleared* if it's classified as terminal-success — i.e. terminal **and** considered a successful conclusion. Plainly, "the dependency completed successfully (or was cancelled cleanly — see below)." Anything else is either *failed* (terminal-non-success) or *gating* (non-terminal).

**Decision:** read phase classification from writ-type config rather than hard-coding `{completed, cancelled}`. A phase counts as cleared if it has both `terminal: true` and `success: true` in its writ-type-config classification. This generalizes cleanly when new writ types onboard, and is consistent with how `resolveActiveTargetPhase` already works (per c-moivk7pd's resolution of Concern 1).

**Cancellation as success:** for v0, treat `cancelled` as terminal-success (mirroring Spider's existing released set). Cancelling a dependency is intentional, and there's no semantic reason for a dependent petition to be poisoned just because someone cancelled the dependency. Revisit if a real workflow demands distinguishing cancelled-as-success from cancelled-as-abandonment.

**No auto-decline on failed dependencies (recoverability over termination).** Even when a target is terminally failed, the Reckoner does **not** decline the dependent petition. Instead, it defers with `defer_reason = 'dependency_failed'`. The dependent writ stays in `new` (recoverable), the petitioner gets a clear signal via the Reckonings log and the staleness diagnostic, and recovery options remain open:

- The petitioner re-petitions after fixing dependencies (e.g. swapping the failed dep for a substitute, retrying the dep, or removing the link).
- The petitioner withdraws the petition (terminal, petitioner-initiated — already in c-modaqnpt's lifecycle).
- An operator inspects and resolves out-of-band.

Auto-declining was rejected because it terminates the petition (per c-modaqnpt: declined is terminal) and forces re-creation rather than re-petition. The dependent's intent is preserved; only its scheduling is paused.

**`defer_reason` enum extension** (additions to c-modaqnpt's set):

- `dependency_pending` — at least one target is still gating; check again next tick.
- `dependency_failed` — all targets terminal, but at least one failed; staleness diagnostic should escalate fast.

### Q3 — Dangling references: **mirror Spider, treat as still-gated**

**Decision:** if a `depends-on` target writ doesn't resolve, defer with `defer_reason = 'dependency_pending'`. Adopt Spider's existing behavior verbatim.

**Rationale:**
- A patron may create a dependent before its dependency exists (out-of-order authoring, especially with multi-writ Stacks transactions).
- A freshly-created target may not yet be visible to a follower due to consistency lag.
- Treating dangling as decline (or even as `dependency_failed`) bakes failure semantics into a transient condition.

The staleness diagnostic in Q4 catches *persistently* dangling references.

### Q4 — Cycles: **no explicit detection; rely on deferral + staleness diagnostic**

**Decision:** the Reckoner does not detect cycles in its consideration pass. Cycles are handled implicitly by the deferral mechanism (every member of a cycle has a non-terminal dep → every member stays deferred → WIP cap is preserved).

Spider continues to fail-loud on cycles post-accept as defense-in-depth, but it should rarely trigger if the Reckoner is doing its job (deferred petitions never reach accept).

**Safety net:** a separate **deferred-petition staleness diagnostic** surfaces any petition with `defer_count > N` or `first_deferred_at > T ago`. This single diagnostic catches:

- Cycles (members defer forever).
- Persistently dangling references (target never appears).
- `dependency_failed` petitions awaiting petitioner action.
- Any other "stuck in deferred" pathology.

**Status:** this mechanism does not yet exist. New click to be opened (see Coordination below).

## Coordination

- **c-moiwnzw6 (depends-on rename):** the Reckoner can land first reading `spider.follows`. Switch to `depends-on` reads after the rename; during the migration window, read either kind (Spider already does this — share the helper).
- **c-moiw5wkv (tick relay):** the dependency check is part of the per-petition evaluation invoked by the tick relay. No new infrastructure needed beyond extending the evaluator.
- **c-modaqnpt (defer metadata):** add `dependency_pending` and `dependency_failed` to the `defer_reason` enum. No other shape changes.
- **New click — staleness diagnostic:** open a sibling click to design the deferred-petition staleness surface (Lattice pulse? Reckonings query? vision-keeper notification? threshold config?). Q4's correctness depends on this existing eventually; without it, `dependency_failed` and cycle pathologies have no escalation path.

## Open items flagged back to Sean

1. **Cancelled-as-success** — confirmed for v0 (matches Spider). Doc reflects this.
2. **No auto-decline on failed deps** — confirmed; recovery preference drove this. Doc reflects this.
3. **Staleness diagnostic** — new click to be opened (Sean's call).

## Sketch of the consideration loop

for petition in pending_petitions:
  links = clerk.linksFrom(petition.writId, kind in ['spider.follows', 'depends-on'])
  if links.isEmpty:
    proceedToCapacityCheck(petition)
    continue

  states = []
  for link in links:
    target = clerk.tryGet(link.targetId)
    if target is None:
      states.append('gating')  # dangling = gating (Q3)
      continue
    classification = writTypeConfig(target.type).phaseClass(target.phase)
    if classification.terminal and classification.success:
      states.append('cleared')
    elif classification.terminal:
      states.append('failed')
    else:
      states.append('gating')

  if all(s == 'cleared' for s in states):
    proceedToCapacityCheck(petition)
  elif any(s == 'gating' for s in states):
    defer(petition, reason='dependency_pending')
  else:
    # all terminal, at least one failed
    defer(petition, reason='dependency_failed')
```

## Next steps

1. Sean reviews and annotates this doc.
2. Open the staleness-diagnostic click.
3. On approval, this becomes the basis for a brief commissioning the Reckoner-side implementation. Implementation work blocks on c-moiwnzw6 only insofar as the link-kind reader needs to handle the dual-kind window cleanly; otherwise it can ship in parallel.

- A patron may create a dependent before its dependency exists (out-of-order authoring, especially with multi-writ Stacks transactions).
- A freshly-created target may not yet be visible to a follower due to consistency lag.
- Treating dangling as decline (or even as `dependency_failed`) bakes failure semantics into a transient condition.

The staleness diagnostic in Q4 catches *persistently* dangling references.

### Q4 — Cycles: **no explicit detection; rely on deferral + staleness diagnostic**

**Decision:** the Reckoner does not detect cycles in its consideration pass. Cycles are handled implicitly by the deferral mechanism (every member of a cycle has a non-terminal dep → every member stays deferred → WIP cap is preserved).

Spider continues to fail-loud on cycles post-accept as defense-in-depth, but it should rarely trigger if the Reckoner is doing its job (deferred petitions never reach accept).

**Safety net:** a separate **deferred-petition staleness diagnostic** surfaces any petition with `defer_count > N` or `first_deferred_at > T ago`. This single diagnostic catches:

- Cycles (members defer forever).
- Persistently dangling references (target never appears).
- `dependency_failed` petitions awaiting petitioner action.
- Any other "stuck in deferred" pathology.

**Status:** this mechanism does not yet exist. New click to be opened (see Coordination below).

## Coordination

- **c-moiwnzw6 (depends-on rename):** prerequisite — assumed shipped before implementation begins. The Reckoner reads `kind = 'depends-on'` from day one; no transition-window logic needed.
- **c-moiw5wkv (tick relay):** the dependency check is part of the per-petition evaluation invoked by the tick relay. No new infrastructure needed beyond extending the evaluator.
- **c-modaqnpt (defer metadata):** add `dependency_pending` and `dependency_failed` to the `defer_reason` enum. No other shape changes.
- **c-moixpj1l (deferred-petition staleness diagnostic):** child click. Q4's correctness depends on this existing eventually; without it, `dependency_failed` and cycle pathologies have no escalation path.

## Open items flagged back to Sean

1. **Cancelled-as-success** — confirmed for v0 (matches Spider). Doc reflects this.
2. **No auto-decline on failed deps** — confirmed; recovery preference drove this. Doc reflects this.
3. **Staleness diagnostic** — new click to be opened (Sean's call).

## Sketch of the consideration loop

```
for petition in pending_petitions:
  links = clerk.linksFrom(petition.writId, kind in ['spider.follows', 'depends-on'])
  if links.isEmpty:
    proceedToCapacityCheck(petition)
    continue

  states = []
  for link in links:
    target = clerk.tryGet(link.targetId)
    if target is None:
      states.append('gating')  # dangling = gating (Q3)
      continue
    classification = writTypeConfig(target.type).phaseClass(target.phase)
    if classification.terminal and classification.success:
      states.append('cleared')
    elif classification.terminal:
      states.append('failed')
    else:
      states.append('gating')

  if all(s == 'cleared' for s in states):
    proceedToCapacityCheck(petition)
  elif any(s == 'gating' for s in states):
    defer(petition, reason='dependency_pending')
  else:
    # all terminal, at least one failed
    defer(petition, reason='dependency_failed')
```

## Next steps

1. Sean reviews and annotates this doc.
2. Open the staleness-diagnostic click.
3. On approval, this becomes the basis for a brief commissioning the Reckoner-side implementation. Implementation work blocks on c-moiwnzw6 only insofar as the link-kind reader needs to handle the dual-kind window cleanly; otherwise it can ship in parallel.

# Reckoner dependency-aware consideration (Option A)

**Click:** c-moiwnmoc
**Parent:** c-moivk7pd (resolved Concern 3 — WIP-cap deadlock)
**Coordinates with:** c-moiw5wkv (tick relay), c-modaqnpt (defer metadata)
**Sequencing assumption:** the depends-on rename (c-moiwnzw6) lands before this work begins implementation, so the Reckoner reads `kind = 'depends-on'` from the start.

## Problem

Without dependency-aware consideration, the Reckoner can accept a batch of dependent writs whose dependencies are still pending petitions, fill the WIP cap with gated writs sitting in `open`, and starve the dependencies of acceptance capacity. The accepted-but-blocked writs hold WIP slots indefinitely while the dependencies they need can't get past the petition gate.

**Resolution shape (Option A from c-moivk7pd):** the Reckoner reads outbound `depends-on` links during consideration. If any direct target is not yet *cleared*, the petition is deferred (writ stays in `new`, doesn't count against WIP). Dependents naturally release in topological order as their dependencies clear.

## Mechanism

On each Reckoner tick, for every pending petition:

1. Load the writ's outbound `depends-on` links via the Clerk's link store (`kind = 'depends-on'`).
2. For each target writ:
   - Resolve the target's current phase.
   - Look up the phase's classification in the writ-type config.
   - Classify as `cleared`, `failed`, or `gating` (see Q2 below).
3. Aggregate (failed-state takes precedence to surface problems faster):
   - All targets `cleared` → petition is eligible (proceed to normal acceptance evaluation).
   - Any target `failed` → defer with `defer_reason = 'dependency_failed'` (regardless of whether other targets are still gating; we want the failed-dep signal to surface immediately for petitioner action).
   - All remaining targets `gating` (none failed) → defer with `defer_reason = 'dependency_pending'`.
4. Emit a Reckonings row for every consideration outcome (existing infra from c-modc7m16).

The pass is stateless — same inputs always produce same outputs. Re-evaluation each tick reuses this logic with no carry-over.

## Resolutions to open questions

### Q1 — Wake-up semantics: **polling, no CDC**

**Decision:** dependency-resolved petitions wake up on the next Reckoner tick. No event-driven triggers.

**Rationale:** c-mod9a54n already concluded that the v0 Reckoner runs on a fixed-interval polling tick — no event-driven triggers — for substrate uniformity with Spider. Dependency wake-up is just another defer reason that gets re-evaluated each tick; it doesn't deserve a special path.

c-modaqnpt's `defer_signal` field is reserved for a future event-based wake-up but is not wired in v0. Worst-case latency is one tick (60s default) between a dependency clearing and a dependent being accepted. For autonomous dependency-graph progression, that's acceptable — we're not blocking patron interaction.

If polling-induced latency becomes a real problem, `defer_signal` is ready to wire up later.

### Q2 — Phase classification: **mirror Spider, but read from writ-type config**

**Terminology:** a target phase is *cleared* if it's classified as terminal-success — i.e. terminal **and** considered a successful conclusion. Plainly, "the dependency completed successfully (or was cancelled cleanly — see below)." Anything else is either *failed* (terminal-non-success) or *gating* (non-terminal).

**Decision:** read phase classification from writ-type config rather than hard-coding `{completed, cancelled}`. A phase counts as cleared if it has both `terminal: true` and `success: true` in its writ-type-config classification. This generalizes cleanly when new writ types onboard, and is consistent with how `resolveActiveTargetPhase` already works (per c-moivk7pd's resolution of Concern 1).

**Cancellation as success:** for v0, treat `cancelled` as terminal-success (mirroring Spider's existing released set). Cancelling a dependency is intentional, and there's no semantic reason for a dependent petition to be poisoned just because someone cancelled the dependency. Revisit if a real workflow demands distinguishing cancelled-as-success from cancelled-as-abandonment.

**No auto-decline on failed dependencies (recoverability over termination).** Even when a target is terminally failed, the Reckoner does **not** decline the dependent petition. Instead, it defers with `defer_reason = 'dependency_failed'`. The dependent writ stays in `new` (recoverable), the petitioner gets a clear signal via the Reckonings log and the staleness diagnostic, and recovery options remain open:

- The petitioner re-petitions after fixing dependencies (e.g. swapping the failed dep for a substitute, retrying the dep, or removing the link).
- The petitioner withdraws the petition (terminal, petitioner-initiated — already in c-modaqnpt's lifecycle).
- An operator inspects and resolves out-of-band.

Auto-declining was rejected because it terminates the petition (per c-modaqnpt: declined is terminal) and forces re-creation rather than re-petition. The dependent's intent is preserved; only its scheduling is paused.

**`defer_reason` enum extension** (additions to c-modaqnpt's set):

- `dependency_pending` — at least one target is still gating; check again next tick.
- `dependency_failed` — at least one target reached a terminal-non-success phase; staleness diagnostic should escalate fast. Takes precedence over `dependency_pending` when both conditions hold.

### Q3 — Dangling references: **mirror Spider, treat as still-gated**

**Decision:** if a `depends-on` target writ doesn't resolve, defer with `defer_reason = 'dependency_pending'`. Adopt Spider's existing behavior verbatim.

**Rationale:**
- A patron may create a dependent before its dependency exists (out-of-order authoring, especially with multi-writ Stacks transactions).
- A freshly-created target may not yet be visible to a follower due to consistency lag.
- Treating dangling as decline (or even as `dependency_failed`) bakes failure semantics into a transient condition.

The staleness diagnostic in Q4 catches *persistently* dangling references.

### Q4 — Cycles: **no explicit detection; rely on deferral + staleness diagnostic**

**Decision:** the Reckoner does not detect cycles in its consideration pass. Cycles are handled implicitly by the deferral mechanism (every member of a cycle has a non-terminal dep → every member stays deferred → WIP cap is preserved).

Spider continues to fail-loud on cycles post-accept as defense-in-depth, but it should rarely trigger if the Reckoner is doing its job (deferred petitions never reach accept).

**Safety net:** a separate **deferred-petition staleness diagnostic** surfaces any petition with `defer_count > N` or `first_deferred_at > T ago`. This single diagnostic catches:

- Cycles (members defer forever).
- Persistently dangling references (target never appears).
- `dependency_failed` petitions awaiting petitioner action.
- Any other "stuck in deferred" pathology.

**Status:** this mechanism does not yet exist. New click to be opened (see Coordination below).

## Coordination

- **c-moiwnzw6 (depends-on rename):** prerequisite — assumed shipped before implementation begins. The Reckoner reads `kind = 'depends-on'` from day one; no transition-window logic needed.
- **c-moiw5wkv (tick relay):** the dependency check is part of the per-petition evaluation invoked by the tick relay. No new infrastructure needed beyond extending the evaluator.
- **c-modaqnpt (defer metadata):** add `dependency_pending` and `dependency_failed` to the `defer_reason` enum. No other shape changes.
- **c-moixpj1l (deferred-petition staleness diagnostic):** child click. Q4's correctness depends on this existing eventually; without it, `dependency_failed` and cycle pathologies have no escalation path.

## Open items flagged back to Sean

1. **Cancelled-as-success** — confirmed for v0 (matches Spider). Doc reflects this.
2. **No auto-decline on failed deps** — confirmed; recovery preference drove this. Doc reflects this.
3. **Staleness diagnostic** — new click to be opened (Sean's call).

## Sketch of the consideration loop

```
for petition in pending_petitions:
  links = clerk.linksFrom(petition.writId, kind = 'depends-on')
  if links.isEmpty:
    proceedToCapacityCheck(petition)
    continue

  states = []
  for link in links:
    target = clerk.tryGet(link.targetId)
    if target is None:
      states.append('gating')  # dangling = gating (Q3)
      continue
    classification = writTypeConfig(target.type).phaseClass(target.phase)
    if classification.terminal and classification.success:
      states.append('cleared')
    elif classification.terminal:
      states.append('failed')
    else:
      states.append('gating')

  if any(s == 'failed' for s in states):
    # failed-state takes precedence — surface fast for petitioner action
    defer(petition, reason='dependency_failed')
  elif any(s == 'gating' for s in states):
    defer(petition, reason='dependency_pending')
  else:
    # all cleared
    proceedToCapacityCheck(petition)
```

## Next steps

2. Open the staleness-diagnostic click.
3. On approval, this becomes the basis for a brief commissioning the Reckoner-side implementation. Implementation work blocks on c-moiwnzw6 only insofar as the link-kind reader needs to handle the dual-kind window cleanly; otherwise it can ship in parallel.

1	---
2	description: Draft, refine, and post pre-Astrolabe briefs. Invoke when asked to draft, write, refine, or post a brief / commission.
3	---
4	
5	# Briefs — Drafting and Dispatching Workflow
6	
7	A **brief** is a pre-Astrolabe artifact that captures the *intent* of a unit of work and the *non-negotiable decisions* that constrain how it gets implemented. Briefs are the input to a sage commission; the sage (Astrolabe) consumes the brief and produces a spec, which the implementing artificer turns into code.
8	
9	## Role chain
10	
11	| Artifact | Author | Audience | Carries |
12	|---|---|---|---|
13	| Brief | Coco (with patron) | The sage (Astrolabe) | Design intent + non-negotiable decisions + scope fences + references to source clicks |
14	| Spec | The sage | The implementing artificer | Implementation plan, file-level changes, API signatures, test plan |
15	| Code | The artificer | Future agents reading the codebase | The actual implementation |
16	
17	A brief that drifts into spec territory leaks decisions that should have been left to the implementer's judgment, makes the brief brittle to refactoring (file:line refs go stale), and collapses the role boundary that the guild's organization depends on. **The discipline is intent, not how-to.**
18	
19	## Content discipline
20	
21	### What a brief carries
22	
23	- **Intent** — what this unit of work accomplishes, framed as motivation + design summary.
24	- **Non-negotiable decisions** — the design choices the implementer cannot revisit. Each should be the conclusion of a click (or set of clicks) from the design subtree.
25	- **Scope fences** — explicit out-of-scope items, especially ones the implementer might naturally drift into.
26	- **References to source clicks** — sages have click access and will resolve references to inline the substantive content when generating the spec. Click ids in briefs are fine; click ids in *specs* are not (specs are consumed by the artificer, who has no click access).
27	
28	### What a brief does not carry
29	
30	- **File paths and line numbers.** "In `clerk.ts:247-264`, replace X with Y" — that's the spec's job. The sage will figure out the right files and lines from the intent.
31	- **Exact API signatures or full type definitions.** A short shape sketch is fine when it's the cleanest way to express intent; full code blocks belong to the spec.
32	- **Numbered §1/§2/§3 implementation plans.** Briefs organize around design topics and decisions, not implementation steps.
33	- **Test file paths, helper names, mock setup, or assertion mechanics.**
34	- **Exit criteria that name specific files** ("`docs/architecture/foo.md` exists") — bake the artifact into the design intent if it matters, otherwise let the sage decide where it lives.
35	- **Commodity constraints** ("lint passes," "all tests pass") — those are always true and add noise.
36	- **Exit criteria that restate the design section verbatim** — redundant.
37	
38	### The nuanced cases
39	
40	Some content sits between intent and implementation. Apply the *gut check*: **is this design intent, or am I doing the spec's job?**
41	
42	**Scenarios to verify** — briefs **may** include a list of behavioral cases that must hold ("writ in a 3-cycle transitions to `stuck` with all members listed"; "two failed blockers gets a resolution naming both short-ids"). Frame these as *behavioral cases the design depends on*, never as test prescriptions ("add a test in `spider.test.ts` that mocks…"). Especially valuable when the design conversation surfaced non-obvious edges (cycles, recovery paths, composition rules) that the implementer might not naturally surface. For simple briefs where the scenarios are obvious from the design text, omit.
43	
44	**Behavioral exit criteria** — briefs **may** include a list of "after this lands, X must be true" checks when the design has multiple parts and a checklist helps confirm coverage. Frame these as *observable outcomes*, never as artifact-existence checks. If the brief has only a few decisions, the design section already implies the outcomes and the checklist is redundant.
45	
46	The line is intent vs how-to, not test/no-test or criteria/no-criteria.
47	
48	## Workflow
49	
50	### Drafting
51	
52	**Default: post the brief directly as a draft-phase writ.** No scratch files, no editor dance. Sean reviews the draft in Oculus; if it needs changes, iterate via `nsg writ edit` (or re-post and cancel the old draft). The draft-phase writ is the reviewable surface.
53	
54	```bash
55	./bin/commission.sh --codex <codex> --draft -- 'body text here'
56	```
57	
58	The body is the full brief, including its markdown. The title is extracted from the first line (the `# Title` heading).
59	
60	**Do not draft in `.scratch/` by default.** The "draft a markdown file, get Sean to review it in his editor, iterate in chat, then post" cycle is dead — Sean explicitly asked to skip it. The only exception is when Sean specifically requests offline editor review of a file (e.g., for a large, complex brief where annotation-in-place is the right collaboration mode). In that case, use `.scratch/brief-<slug>.md`; otherwise, direct-to-system.
61	
62	A typical brief structure (adapt as needed; this is a writing template, not a file-location directive):
63	
64	```
65	# <Title>
66	
67	## Intent
68	<one or two paragraphs: what this commission accomplishes, the gist of the design>
69	
70	## Motivation
71	<why now, what's broken or missing, who/what is the natural consumer>
72	
73	## Non-negotiable decisions
74	<organized by decision, not by file. Each section is one design choice
75	with a brief rationale. Reference the source click for each.>
76	
77	## Out of scope
78	<explicit fences: things the implementer might drift into, with a
79	one-line reason for each exclusion>
80	
81	## References
82	<the design subtree (parent click), supporting clicks, prior briefs
83	this one builds on or supersedes>
84	```
85	
86	Other sections appear when the work warrants them — e.g., "Substrate changes" when the brief carries adjustments to existing substrate alongside the main work; "Authoring surfaces" when there are multiple consumer-facing entry points.
87	
88	### Draft phase vs immediate dispatch
89	
90	The direct-to-system workflow gives you two dispatch modes:
91	
92	- **Draft (`--draft`)** — the default when any of the following apply: the brief is part of a multi-commission batch, depends on other writs (via `spider.follows`), or Sean hasn't yet seen the final body. Sean reviews in Oculus; publish via `nsg writ publish --id <writ-id>` when ready.
93	- **Open (no `--draft`)** — appropriate when the brief was fully worked out in chat, is a single unit, has no dependencies, and Sean has signaled he wants it to go immediately. The writ enters the dispatch queue at post time.
94	
95	When in doubt, use `--draft`. A draft is cheap to review and publish; an already-dispatched writ is harder to retract.
96	
97	### Iteration
98	
99	Sean iterates on a draft by reviewing it in Oculus and telling Coco what to change. When he provides feedback — edits, annotations, or out-of-band comments — **restate a summary of that feedback in chat**, using Sean's direct words as much as possible. This "states it for the record" so the substance appears in the transcript where Scribe can capture it (transcript-capture habit, per coco.md). Apply the feedback via `nsg writ edit` (for narrow body tweaks) or by re-posting a corrected draft and cancelling the old one (for substantial rewrites).
100	
101	### Click references in briefs
102	
103	Click ids are first-class in briefs — they're how the brief connects to the design conversation. The sage extracts and inlines the substance when generating the spec. Don't strip click ids out of briefs to "make them self-contained"; that's the sage's job, not Coco's.
104	
105	### Stay inside the target repository
106	
107	A brief is posted as the body of a writ in a specific codex, and every downstream reader (the sage, the artificer, the reviewer) operates inside the codex's target repository. **Do not reference any path or artifact that lives outside that repository** — in particular, nothing under the sanctum (`/workspace/nexus-mk2/...`), nothing under `.scratch/`, nothing under `experiments/`, nothing in sibling repos. Those paths are dead links from the artificer's perspective.
108	
109	This applies to:
110	
111	- File path references (`.scratch/brief-foo.md`, `experiments/X008/spec.md`, etc.) — omit entirely.
112	- Cross-commission references — when pointing at prior or parallel briefs, name them by intent ("the link-substrate rename sweep commission") rather than by scratch path. If the relationship is load-bearing, the brief should stand on its own intent without requiring the reader to fetch a sibling artifact.
113	- Sanctum-side documentation, data, or tooling paths — not available to agents operating in the framework repo.
114	
115	Click ids are the exception — clicks live in the guild's books and are resolvable by any sage with click access regardless of which repository the commission targets.
116	
117	When drafting, do the grep before handing the brief to Sean: `grep -n '\.scratch\|nexus-mk2\|sanctum' brief-*.md` should return nothing.
118	
119	## Posting
120	
121	Use `bin/commission.sh` from the sanctum:
122	
123	    ./bin/commission.sh --codex <codex> [--draft] -- 'body text...'
124	
125	Or, when a scratch file exists (offline-review exception):
126	
127	    ./bin/commission.sh --codex <codex> [--draft] -- @.scratch/brief-<slug>.md
128	
129	- `--codex` is required. Common values: `nexus` (the framework). Ask Sean if uncertain.
130	- `--draft` creates the writ in draft phase (does not dispatch until published). See "Draft phase vs immediate dispatch" above for when to use it.
131	- The `--` form takes the body as a literal argument; the `@<path>` form reads the body from a file. In both cases, the title is auto-extracted from the brief's first heading.
132	- The script returns the writ id (`w-…`) on success. Capture it for any follow-on work (follows-links, publish, bookkeeping).
133	
134	Underlying CLI: `bin/commission.sh` wraps `nsg commission-post`. Always prefer the wrapper for consistency.
135	
136	### Multi-commission batches
137	
138	When dispatching multiple related commissions (e.g., a decomposed refactor with dependencies), post all of them as drafts, wire the `spider.follows` links between them, then leave them draft for Sean's Oculus review. Publish the leaves last, once Sean has ratified the batch. Link syntax:
139	
140	    nsg writ link --source-id <follower> --target-id <prerequisite> --kind spider.follows --label follows
141	
142	Both `--label` and `--kind` are required — `--label` is the casual relationship name; `--kind` is the load-bearing registered link type that Spider's dispatch logic reads.
143	
144	## Post-dispatch bookkeeping
145	
146	"Dispatch" means the writ enters the Spider's queue — at post time for open-phase writs, at publish time for draft-phase writs. The bookkeeping fires on the actual dispatch moment.
147	
148	Three things follow:
149	
150	1. **Conclude the parent design click.** The click whose subtree drove the design is now resolved by the act of dispatch. Conclude it with a short summary of the final shape and the dispatched writ id(s). Example conclusion:
151	
152	       Design fully resolved and dispatched as commission w-mo35s0fo-1a1e3cd285bc.
153	       Final shape: <one-paragraph summary of the locked-in design>.
154	
155	2. **Delete any scratch file.** If the offline-review exception was used and a `.scratch/brief-*.md` file was created, delete it after dispatch — the writ carries the content now. In the default direct-to-system flow, there's nothing to delete.
156	
157	3. **Coco-log entry.** The dispatch is part of a session of work; the coco-log entry covers the design conversation and the act of dispatching. Reference the writ id(s) in the log so future sessions can join the design session to the resulting commission(s).
158	
159	For multi-commission batches, conclude the parent click once (naming all dispatched writ ids) rather than per-commission.
160	
161	## Common pitfalls
162	
163	- **Drifting into spec.** The most common failure. Symptoms: file paths appearing in the brief, full TypeScript code blocks beyond a shape sketch, test file names, exit criteria like "the foo.ts file at line N is updated." When you catch this, ask the gut-check question and rewrite.
164	- **Drafting in `.scratch/` by default.** The old editor-review dance is dead. Post directly as a draft-phase writ and let Sean review in Oculus; only use `.scratch/` when Sean explicitly asks for offline file review.
165	- **Leaking sanctum references into the brief.** `.scratch/...` paths, sanctum doc paths, experiment directories. Dead links from the artificer's perspective. See "Stay inside the target repository" above.
166	- **Stripping click references.** Don't try to make briefs self-contained by inlining the substance of their source clicks — that's the sage's job. Briefs reference; specs inline.
167	- **Skipping the wrapper script.** Always go through `bin/commission.sh` for consistency with the dispatch flow.
168	- **Forgetting `--label` on a `spider.follows` link.** The `nsg writ link` command requires both `--label` (casual name) and `--kind` (registered load-bearing type). Passing only `--kind` fails.
169	- **Forgetting to conclude the parent click.** Leaves the design click sitting in `live` indefinitely, pretending there's still active design work when the work is actually in flight as a commission.
